flashcards.front: What does ACID stand for, and what does each property actually guarantee?
flashcards.back: A — Atomicity: all writes in a transaction succeed or all are aborted. If anything fails mid-transaction, partial writes are rolled back. ('All or nothing') C — Consistency: the application's invariants are maintained (e.g., account debits must be balanced by credits). Actually a property of the APPLICATION, not the database — the database provides the other three, but consistency is up to you. I — Isolation: concurrently executing transactions appear as if they ran serially. One transaction cannot see another transaction's uncommitted data (to varying degrees depending on isolation level). D — Durability: once a transaction is committed, its data will not be lost even in the event of a crash. Typically implemented via write-ahead log (WAL) flushed to disk before acknowledging commit.

flashcards.front: Describe the four common transaction isolation levels and the anomalies each prevents or permits.
flashcards.back: 1. Read Uncommitted: transactions can read uncommitted writes from other transactions (dirty reads). Prevents: nothing meaningful. Used rarely. 2. Read Committed: prevents dirty reads (only see committed data) and dirty writes (don't overwrite uncommitted data). Common default (PostgreSQL, Oracle). Still allows non-repeatable reads (data changes between two reads in same tx) and phantoms. 3. Snapshot Isolation (Repeatable Read): each transaction reads from a consistent snapshot taken at transaction start. Prevents dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, and most phantoms. Implemented with MVCC (multi-version concurrency control) — reads don't block writers; writers don't block readers. Still allows write skew anomalies. Default in PostgreSQL ('repeatable read'), MySQL InnoDB. 4. Serializable: strongest — transactions execute as if completely serial. Prevents all anomalies including write skew. Implemented via: actual serial execution (VoltDB, Redis), two-phase locking (2PL, older DBs), or Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI, modern — optimistic approach).

flashcards.front: What is write skew? Give a concrete example and explain why snapshot isolation doesn't prevent it.
flashcards.back: Write skew: two transactions read the same data, each makes a decision based on what it read, and each writes to different objects. The combined result violates an invariant that neither transaction individually violated. Example (on-call doctors): Transaction A reads: 2 doctors on call (Alice, Bob). Invariant: at least 1 must be on call. A decides to remove Alice (still 1 left). Transaction B simultaneously reads: 2 doctors on call. B decides to remove Bob (still 1 left). Both commit. Result: 0 doctors on call — invariant violated. Why snapshot isolation fails: A and B each read a snapshot where both doctors are on call. Neither sees the other's write. Each transaction is individually valid from its snapshot's perspective. Snapshot isolation only sees a consistent snapshot at START of transaction — it doesn't see writes made by concurrent transactions during execution. Solution: serializable isolation (e.g., SSI or 2PL), or materializing the conflict (add a row for 'on-call slot' that both transactions write to, turning write skew into a write-write conflict detectable by snapshot isolation).

flashcards.front: Compare Two-Phase Locking (2PL) and Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI) for achieving serializability.
flashcards.back: Two-Phase Locking (2PL) — pessimistic: readers acquire shared locks; writers acquire exclusive locks. A writer blocks all readers and writers; a reader blocks writers (but not other readers). Phase 1 (expanding): acquire locks as needed. Phase 2 (shrinking): release locks only after commit/abort. Pros: prevents all anomalies; no conflicts — all serialized. Cons: poor throughput under contention; deadlocks (transactions waiting for each other's locks — need detection and rollback); readers block writers and vice versa. Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI) — optimistic: transactions proceed with snapshot reads (no blocking). At commit time, SSI detects if any transaction read data that was modified by a concurrent transaction (detects 'stale reads'). If so, abort and retry. Pros: reads never block writes; much better throughput for read-heavy workloads. Cons: higher abort rate under high contention (wasted work on transactions that will be retried); requires tracking read sets. Used by: PostgreSQL (serializable isolation level since v9.1), FoundationDB, CockroachDB.

flashcards.front: In practice, why do most applications use snapshot isolation rather than full serializability, and when is this dangerous?
flashcards.back: Why most use snapshot isolation (not serializability): Serializability (2PL) has significant performance cost — lock contention slows throughput, deadlocks require retries, readers block writers. SSI is better but still has abort overhead. Snapshot isolation is fast and prevents most common anomalies (dirty reads, non-repeatable reads) with MVCC — readers and writers don't block each other. Dangerous cases where snapshot isolation is insufficient: (1) Write skew scenarios (see on-call doctor example) — common in booking systems, constraint enforcement, account balance checks. (2) Phantoms: a transaction reads a set of rows matching a condition; another transaction inserts rows matching that condition; first transaction makes decisions based on incomplete picture. (3) Any multi-object invariant that spans multiple rows/tables. Rule: if your application has invariants that involve reads and writes across multiple objects by concurrent transactions, snapshot isolation is not enough — use serializable isolation or redesign to avoid the race condition (pessimistic locks, atomic compare-and-set operations, or serializable DBs).

flashcards.front: Design a hotel room booking system that prevents double-booking, where two users simultaneously try to book the last available room.
flashcards.back: Race condition: User A reads available rooms → 1 room left → decides to book. User B reads available rooms → 1 room left → decides to book. Both commit → double-booked. Solutions by isolation level: Read committed (no fix): both transactions can commit — classic double-booking. Snapshot isolation (no fix): each transaction sees snapshot at start — both see 1 room available, both book. Write skew anomaly. Serializability (fixes): with 2PL, one booking transaction acquires an exclusive lock; the other waits. With SSI, one transaction detects its snapshot was stale (other committed a booking) and aborts. Application-level fixes (if stuck with weaker isolation): (1) SELECT FOR UPDATE: explicitly lock the booking row before reading — 'SELECT * FROM rooms WHERE id=? FOR UPDATE'. Forces sequential execution. (2) Materialized conflict: insert a 'reservation attempt' row for the room+date — two attempts create a write-write conflict detectable by snapshot isolation. (3) Atomic compare-and-set: UPDATE rooms SET booked=true WHERE id=? AND booked=false; check affected rows count. Fails atomically if another transaction already booked. Best practice: use a DB that supports serializable isolation (PostgreSQL, CockroachDB) and enable it for booking transactions — accept slightly lower throughput for correctness guarantees.

puzzles.title: Match Isolation Level to Anomaly It Prevents
puzzles.instructions: Match each isolation level to the anomalies it prevents (cumulative — higher levels prevent all below).

pairs.right: Non-repeatable reads, most read phantoms (via MVCC)
Write skew, phantoms, all concurrency anomalies

puzzles.hint: Each level builds on the previous. Snapshot isolation uses MVCC to prevent reads changing within a transaction.
puzzles.explanation: Isolation levels form a hierarchy: read uncommitted < read committed < snapshot isolation < serializable. Each level prevents a superset of the previous level's anomaly prevention.

puzzles.title: Two-Phase Locking — Write Skew Prevention
puzzles.instructions: Arrange what happens when two concurrent transactions attempt to remove themselves from the on-call schedule using 2PL.

steps.text: Tx A: SELECT doctors on call — acquires shared lock on on-call rows (Alice, Bob)
Tx B: SELECT doctors on call — also acquires shared lock (shared locks are compatible)
Tx A: decides to remove Alice — tries to acquire EXCLUSIVE lock on Alice's row
Tx B holds shared lock on Alice's row — Tx A BLOCKS waiting for Tx B to release
Tx B: decides to remove Bob — tries to acquire exclusive lock on Bob's row — SUCCEEDS
Tx B commits, releases all locks; now only Alice is on call
Tx A unblocks, acquires exclusive lock on Alice's row — reads: only 1 doctor on call now
Tx A sees invariant would be violated (0 doctors) — aborts

puzzles.hint: Shared locks are compatible; exclusive locks are not. The key is when Tx A re-reads after being unblocked.
puzzles.explanation: 2PL prevents write skew by blocking Tx A from writing until Tx B commits. When Tx A re-reads after being unblocked, it sees Tx B's committed state and correctly identifies the invariant violation.

puzzles.title: MVCC Read Visibility
puzzles.instructions: Fill in the blanks about MVCC snapshot isolation.
puzzles.question: Under MVCC, each transaction sees a {{snapshot}} of the database taken at transaction {{start}}. Writes create {{new_versions}} rather than overwriting. Readers never {{block}} writers and writers never block readers.
puzzles.hint: MVCC keeps old row versions until no transaction can see them.
puzzles.explanation: MVCC = Multi-Version Concurrency Control. Each write appends a new version with a transaction ID. Readers pick versions visible at their snapshot timestamp.
